questions.question: Briefly explain the difference between 'Inferential Adequacy' and 'Inferential Efficiency'. (3 marks)
expectedPoints: Inferential Adequacy is about the *capability* to derive new knowledge correctly.
Inferential Efficiency is about the *computational ease and speed* of deriving that knowledge.
A system can be adequate (can derive all necessary conclusions) but inefficient (takes an impractically long time to do so).

questions.question: Discuss the Declarative vs. Procedural knowledge representation debate. Explain each paradigm, its advantages, disadvantages, and why a hybrid approach is often used in modern AI systems. (10 marks)
expectedPoints: Definition of Declarative Knowledge: Focus on 'what' - facts, truths about the world. Represented by logic, semantic networks. Advantages: Modular, readable, easier to maintain and modify.
Definition of Procedural Knowledge: Focus on 'how' - processes, algorithms. Represented by rules, code. Advantages: Highly efficient for specific tasks, can encode heuristics.
Disadvantages of Declarative: Can lead to slow, undirected inference. May not capture procedural knowledge well.
Disadvantages of Procedural: Knowledge is tangled with control logic, making it hard to understand, verify, and update.

Hybrid Approach: Modern systems combine both. A declarative knowledge base stores facts, and a separate procedural control mechanism (an inference engine) uses strategies to efficiently reason over those facts. This balances efficiency with modularity.
